mnemonics.title: ASF - Alphabet Is Always Simple Finite
mnemonics.content: Alphabet (Σ) is a finite non-empty set of symbols. Remember: 'A'lphabet 'S'hould 'F'inally contain finite symbols - not infinite!

mnemonics.title: SED - String Ends Depends on Alphabet
mnemonics.content: String (w) is a finite sequence of symbols drawn from an Alphabet. Remember: 'S'ymbols 'E'nd 'D'efinitely when written from Σ

mnemonics.title: LIS - Language Is Set
mnemonics.content: Language (L) is a set of strings. Remember: 'L'anguage 'I's simply a 'S'et of strings from Σ*

mnemonics.title: KLEENE - Keep Looping Everything, Recursively Enclosed, Never Ending
mnemonics.content: Kleene Star (Σ*) includes all strings (including ε) formed from alphabet Σ. Σ* = Σ⁰ ∪ Σ¹ ∪ Σ² ∪ ... ∪ Σⁿ

mnemonics.title: EMPTY - Every Mind Produces Empty Yearning
mnemonics.content: Empty string (ε or λ) has length 0 - it contains no symbols but is still a valid string

mnemonics.title: CONCAT - Coming Or Crossing Numbers Adds Things
mnemonics.content: Concatenation operation combines two strings by appending the second to the first. If w = 'abc' and x = 'def', then wx = 'abcdef'

mnemonics.title: POWER - Pieces Of Words Repeated Exponentially
mnemonics.content: Σ^k represents all strings of length k that can be formed from alphabet Σ. For Σ = {0,1}, Σ² = {00,01,10,11}

acronyms.usage: Used to denote an alphabet - the fundamental set of symbols in Theory of Computation
Represents the string of length zero. Used in definitions of operations and closures
Alternative notation for empty string, used in some textbooks
Returns the number of symbols in string w. |ε| = 0
Set of ALL strings (including ε) that can be formed from Σ. Σ* = {ε, a, aa, ab, ba, aaa, ...}
Set of all NON-EMPTY strings from Σ. Σ+ = Σ* - {ε}
Set of all strings of EXACT length k from Σ
String w written in reverse order. If w = 'abc', then w^R = 'cba'
Language containing reverse of each string in L

acronyms.expansion: Kleene Star applied to Language L
acronyms.usage: Language L concatenated with itself zero or more times, including empty string

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a LEGO box containing only specific colored blocks. Each block color represents a symbol. The box (alphabet) is finite - you have only those colors available to build strings.
Think of beads on a necklace. Each bead is a symbol from the alphabet. The necklace (string) has a specific length and order. You can have empty necklace (ε) with no beads.
Picture a dictionary or a word list. The dictionary contains valid words (strings) - that's your language. Invalid words aren't in the language.
Imagine a computer generating ALL possible combinations - single letters, double letters, triple letters, infinitely many! Include the empty combo too (ε).
Concatenation = linking two necklaces end-to-end. Prefix = first few beads. Suffix = last few beads. Substring = any consecutive section of beads.
Count the beads on your necklace. That's the length. Empty necklace has 0 beads.
Think of lock combinations. Σ² for {0,1} is like all 2-digit lock combinations: 00, 01, 10, 11. Each digit can be 0 or 1.

analogies.analogy: Like the English alphabet (26 letters) or keyboard keys. Just as we use 26 letters to form words, in TOC we define our own alphabet to form strings.
Like a word or sentence written using letters from our defined alphabet. 'HELLO' is a string using alphabet {A,B,...,Z}.
Like a dictionary or the set of all valid English sentences. It's a collection of valid strings that follow certain rules.
Like an empty page - nothing written, but the page exists. It's a valid string, not 'nothing' but 'zero symbols'.
Like the 'All Possible Words' list in a dictionary - every single word you can form, plus a blank entry for 'nothing' (ε).
Like joining two train coaches together. Coach A + Coach B = Coach AB. The second coach attaches to the end of the first.
Prefix = your phone number's area code at the start. Suffix = the last 4 digits at the end. Both are parts of the whole string.
Union = combining two word lists. Intersection = words common to both lists. Complement = all valid words NOT in your list.
